name: MemOS (Memory Operating System for LLMs)
activity: 8,300+ GitHub stars, 732+ forks, 86 open issues, 20 open PRs as of 2026-04. Multiple commits per week; v2.0.x patch releases through April 2026. Active contributor base with regular plugin ecosystem updates (OpenClaw, Hermes).
storage_backend: Pluggable: Neo4j (graph, TreeTextMemory), Qdrant (vector, GeneralTextMemory), SQLite (local plugin FTS5+vector), Redis Streams (scheduling). Default reference stack is Neo4j + Qdrant.
representation_primitive: MemCube — a standardized container that encapsulates both a semantic payload and structured metadata (provenance, versioning, type tag). Three concrete primitives live inside MemCubes: (1) GeneralTextMemory — flat vector-based storage for unstructured text with semantic search + metadata filtering; (2) TreeTextMemory — hierarchical graph-style memory combining tree hierarchy and cross-branch links for dynamic knowledge graphs, typically Neo4j-backed with multi-hop reasoning support; (3) KVCacheMemory — activation-level memory holding KV-cache blocks for fast context injection without re-tokenisation. MemCubes can be fused, migrated, and composed across types.
api_shape: Python library (primary SDK), REST API (FastAPI-based, full CRUD on memories), MCP server (first-party, v1.1.2+ with full MCP protocol support), CLI via Python entry points. First-party MCP server: yes.
hosting: Self-host (open-source, quick deploy or full Docker compose). Also MemOS Cloud (managed SaaS) with free tier for OpenClaw plugin. Both modes available.
llm_dependency: LLM required at both ingest and retrieval. Ingest: MemReader parses natural-language requests, identifies memory-related intents, and converts them to structured Memory API chains. Retrieval: LLM used in query expansion and answer synthesis. Default model configurable; examples show OpenAI-compatible APIs; local Ollama supported. Plaintext memory operations can be partially done without LLM using structured writes.
schema_extensibility: LLM-learns + prescribed hybrid. The MemCube abstraction is fixed (standard metadata envelope), but memory type registration is extensible — developers subclass memory types. TreeTextMemory schema evolves via LLM-driven entity extraction. No YAML config file for schema; extension is code-level.
schema_richness: The paper and docs do not enumerate a fixed entity/edge taxonomy comparable to Gimle §5's 20+ entities / 15+ edges target. Core types: MemCube (meta-container), GeneralTextMemory, TreeTextMemory, KVCacheMemory, ParametricMemory (conceptual, weight-level). Edge types in TreeTextMemory: parent-child hierarchy + cross-branch links (count not specified). Multi-label: not explicitly stated. Multi-graph: each user/session has isolated namespaces. Schema richness is significantly below Gimle's code-graph targets; no first-class Symbol, File, PR, or Commit nodes.
temporal_model: Custom lifecycle model: hot/warm/cold tiering with promotion/demotion paths. MemCube metadata includes provenance and versioning fields. Not a formal bi-temporal model (no valid_from/to + recorded_at pair exposed as first-class); timestamps are recorded at ingest time. Eviction/demotion triggers version transitions.
forgetting_policy: OS-page-eviction style: hot memories distilled into parametric weights; stable context promoted to KV-cache; cold/outdated knowledge demoted or archived. v2.0 adds precise deletion API. Semantic-dedup applied during merge. Combination of LRU-like tiering + semantic-dedup + agent-driven-deletion.
ingest_latency_class: Real-time-incremental: v1.1.3 introduced millisecond-level async memory add. Sub-second per episode claimed for plaintext memory; KV-cache injection is inference-time.
retrieval_modes: Hybrid: vector similarity (Qdrant), BM25 full-text search (FTS5 in SQLite local plugin; enhanced BM25 in v1.1.3), graph-traversal (Neo4j, multi-hop via TreeTextMemory), mixture search (v1.1.3 combined BM25 + graph recall). Community search and drift-search not explicitly mentioned. Next-Scene Prediction (v2 paper 2507.03724) proactively preloads relevant memory fragments during inference.
retrieval_agenticity: Proactive/policy-guided: Next-Scene Prediction (2507.03724) predicts upcoming context needs and preloads fragments asynchronously. Standard retrieval is hybrid-lookup (vector + graph). No Self-RAG or CRAG loop described explicitly; multi-hop supported via TreeTextMemory graph traversal.
retrieval_budget_control: No explicit per-query token/hop/edge budget parameters exposed as first-class API. The scheduling layer does async preloading to manage latency, but not a budget-constrained interface.
multi_project_namespace: Yes: multi-user and multi-session memory workflows orchestrated at the scheduling layer. Configuration exposes per-user and per-agent namespaces. MOS_NEO4J_SHARED_DB flag suggests project-label-level partitioning within a shared Neo4j instance. Cross-project queries not explicitly documented.
multi_user_access: Per-agent-namespace + multi-user: scheduling layer orchestrates multi-user, multi-session workflows. OpenClaw plugin (Cloud variant) supports multi-agent memory sharing with 72% lower token usage. RBAC not explicitly described in open-source docs.
embedder_options: Configurable via config: default options include ollama or universal_api backends. Supports text-embedding-v4 (likely Alibaba Qwen family) and OpenAI-compatible embedding APIs (API key + base URL configurable). Custom embedder API base URL supported. Dimensions depend on chosen model; not fixed. Local models supported through Ollama.
llm_client_options: OpenAI-compatible API (configurable base URL + key), Ollama (local). Default model name is configurable. LLM can be partially bypassed for structured writes, but retrieval synthesis typically requires LLM. No explicit structured-writes-only mode documented.
cost_privacy: Self-hosted mode: data stays on-device; no external calls except to configured LLM/embedding endpoints. MemOS Cloud: data sent to managed service. Token cost per ingest not published in papers; v1.1.3 claims millisecond async add with reduced overhead. OpenClaw Cloud plugin claims 72% lower token usage versus baseline. LongMemEval results achieved with unspecified LLM costs.
code_artifact_linkage: None first-party. MemOS is a general-purpose memory OS for conversational and agentic workloads; it has no native concepts of Symbol, File, AST node, PR, Commit, or Call Graph. Code can be stored as plaintext in GeneralTextMemory, and TreeTextMemory can model arbitrary entity hierarchies, but there is no built-in extractor for code repositories. Ranked: file-path possible via manual ingest only; symbol / line-level / issue-PR-to-symbol — not supported.
code_analysis_features: None first-party. No call graph, diff-awareness, symbol index, dependency graph, import resolution, type-aware analysis, or cross-language support. All code analysis would require custom plugins/extractors outside the core MemOS system.
memory_os_layer: All three tiers: parametric (model-weight knowledge, LoRA-style distillation pathway mentioned), activation (KVCacheMemory — first-class KV-cache block management), plaintext (GeneralTextMemory + TreeTextMemory — primary operational tier). Cross-type migration: explicitly supported via MemCube migration paths (hot to KV-cache, plaintext to parametric distillation, cold to archive).
self_improvement_mechanism: LLM-reflection + online-alias-learning hybrid. MemReader translates user inputs into memory operation chains (LLM-driven intent parsing). v2.0 adds memory feedback loop (user signals on memory quality). Tool memory for agent planning enables skill evolution across tasks. Not an ACE-GRC pipeline, but feedback and distillation cycle is analogous in spirit.
provenance_standard: Custom-log: MemCube metadata includes provenance and versioning fields. No W3C-PROV, OpenTelemetry, or MCP-native-trace described in documentation. Audit trail is internal to MemOS metadata.
benchmark_results: From arXiv 2507.03724 (v2 paper, July 2025): MemOS-1031 ranks first across all categories on PreFEval, PersonaMem, LongMemEval, and LoCoMo benchmarks, outperforming MIRIX, Mem0, Zep, Memobase, MemU, and Supermemory. Specific numeric scores not published in accessible search results. Third-party verification status: [uncertain] — results appear in the MemTensor-authored paper; independent replication not confirmed.
strengths: 1. Unified three-tier memory OS: uniquely treats parametric, activation (KV-cache), and plaintext memory under a single MemCube abstraction with migration pathways — no other open-source system covers all three tiers. 2. Production-ready and actively maintained: v2.0+ (Stardust) ships REST API, MCP server, Redis scheduling, multi-modal memory, and tool memory; 8K+ stars with regular releases in 2026. 3. Hybrid retrieval (vector + BM25 + graph multi-hop) with async Next-Scene Prediction preloading — directly applicable to Gimle's need for low-latency context injection across sessions.
weaknesses: 1. No code-artifact intelligence: zero first-party support for Symbol, File, AST, Commit, or PR nodes — Gimle's §5 schema would need to be built entirely on top. 2. Schema below Gimle targets: TreeTextMemory provides a generic KG but no enumerated entity/edge taxonomy near the 20+ entities / 15+ edges target; richness must be hand-built. 3. Temporal model is informal: no bi-temporal (valid_from/to + recorded_at) semantics; only lifecycle tiering and ingest timestamps — insufficient for Gimle's decision/iteration audit requirements.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas. Do not adopt whole — MemOS is a general memory OS without code-graph semantics. Specific ideas to steal: (A) MemCube envelope pattern — wrap every Gimle node (Symbol, File, Decision, etc.) in a metadata envelope with provenance + versioning + type-tag, enabling uniform lifecycle operations (promote, demote, archive, fuse) regardless of node type. Schema sketch: {memcube_id, type: Symbol, valid_from, recorded_at, payload, provenance: {source: extractor.code, sha}}. (B) Three-tier memory OS framing — Gimle can adopt the parametric/activation/plaintext distinction to decide where each node type lives: Symbol/File/PR goes to plaintext (Neo4j); hot execution context goes to activation (KV-cache prefix); learned patterns go to parametric (future fine-tune). (C) Next-Scene Prediction as a retrieval preload pattern — proactively fetch likely-needed context (e.g., files touched in the last iteration) before the LLM makes its next tool call, reducing perceived latency. Incompatible aspects: MemOS's flat GeneralTextMemory and TreeTextMemory do not map to Gimle's faceted 4-axis multi-label schema; bi-temporal semantics must be added from scratch.
